items.title: Humanoid robotics: the supply chain, not the hardware, is the binding constraint
items.idea: Autonomous robots are moving from pilots to real port and warehouse operations, but full autonomy remains years away. McKinsey's thesis: the bottleneck isn't the AI or hardware — it's components that don't yet exist at scale. Suppliers who invest now in humanoid-specific parts and life-cycle revenue models will capture a multibillion-dollar opportunity.
items.angle: Supply-chain scarcity framing for humanoids is a different thesis from any robotics ETF. Analog: EV battery supply chains in 2019.
items.tweetA: Humanoid robots are moving from pilot programs into real warehouses. The bottleneck isn't the AI or the actuators — it's the supply chain for components nobody's built at scale yet. Same playbook as EV batteries 2019, semis 2021. The money is in finding what can't be sourced.
items.tweetB: McKinsey's humanoid framing: the robot is beside the point. The opportunity is for component suppliers who understand where constraints cluster before demand arrives. That's a different investment thesis than anything a robotics ETF currently offers.

items.title: The 'agentic convergence trap': same AI tools = identical decisions, competitive advantage evaporates
items.idea: HBR coins the 'agentic convergence trap': organizations deploying similar AI agents on the same underlying models converge toward identical decisions, workflows, and customer experiences. The moat disappears not through competition but through convergence. McKinsey identifies nine competitive moats — the counter-strategy is proprietary data, company-specific objectives, and measuring whether outputs are converging with competitors'.
items.angle: Reframes the AI adoption risk: it's not being left behind, it's becoming indistinguishable. Bullish for companies with genuinely proprietary data moats.
items.tweetA: HBR calls it the 'agentic convergence trap.' Every company runs Claude on the same enterprise dataset — decisions, workflows, and customer experiences all converge to the same output. The moat evaporates not through competition but through identical tools. Proprietary data is the only defense.
items.tweetB: The AI winner in enterprise isn't who adopts fastest — it's who builds around data competitors can't access. Nine companies running the same model on the same public data produce the same strategy. The companies still worth owning are the ones where the data IS the moat.

items.title: US spin-offs outperform S&P 500 by widest margin in 6 years in Q1 2026
items.idea: US spin-off stocks outperformed the S&P 500 by their widest margin in six years in Q1 2026, reflecting a shift away from diversified conglomerates toward simpler focused businesses. McKinsey: pre-separation restructuring — cost base reset, decision rights, governance overhaul — is the single biggest determinant of post-spin performance.
items.angle: Spin-off alpha is historically front-loaded. Six-year outperformance high suggests the cycle is early. Which conglomerates still carry a diversification discount?
items.tweetA: Spin-offs outperformed the S&P 500 by the widest margin in 6 years in Q1. The market is repricing 'focused over diversified' in real time. Which conglomerates haven't spun yet and are still carrying a diversification discount?
items.tweetB: McKinsey: biggest driver of spin-off success isn't asset quality — it's whether the parent restructured BEFORE the separation. The spin-offs that skip that step are the ones to short post-separation.

items.title: 4-0 Risk-On sweep for third straight week: every intermarket signal stays offensive
items.idea: Lead-Lag's four-signal framework: clean sweep for third week running. Beta Rotation (XLU/SPY RoC -5.97%), Treasury Rotation (TLT -1.23% vs IEF -0.48% in April), Lumber/Gold flipped Risk-On (lumber +2.86%, gold -11.85% over 13 weeks), S&P 500 at 7,473.48 = +10.4% above 200-day SMA. Historically, clean-sweep Risk-On readings cluster in the strongest bull market phases.
items.angle: The Lumber/Gold flip back to risk-on is the most meaningful signal: gold's record run has paused while lumber bounced off $570 — a real-economy demand signal.
items.tweetA: Every intermarket signal pointing offense for 3 straight weeks: defensives sold, duration avoided, lumber beats gold, S&P 10% above 200DMA. Clean 4-0 risk-on sweeps cluster in the strongest bull market phases. You don't chase. You don't fade.
items.tweetB: Gold down 11.85% over 13 weeks while lumber is up 2.86%. The Lumber/Gold ratio flipped back risk-on — that's a real-economy signal. Housing demand expectations recovering off $570 lumber lows. Watch housing starts.

charts.desc: Signal performance charts (paywalled): Beta Rotation $100K growth, Treasury Rotation, Lumber/Gold ratio

items.title: Hassabis: 'foothills of the singularity' — AGI by 2029, current agentic era is 'the practice run'
items.idea: Google DeepMind CEO Demis Hassabis told Axios post-Google I/O that AGI could arrive by 2029 — earlier than prior ~2030 estimates — and that humanity has 'only a few years to prepare.' He called the current agentic era 'a practice run for far more powerful systems.' Looming milestone: recursive self-improvement. He's deliberately using provocative language to pressure governments. Specifically worried economists aren't taking AGI impact seriously enough.
items.angle: Hassabis saying 'foothills of singularity' at Google I/O is a deliberate PR escalation — Google joining Anthropic/OpenAI in using existential language to shape regulatory narrative.
items.tweetA: Hassabis moved his AGI timeline to 'possibly 2029' and called today's agentic systems 'a practice run for far more powerful things.' He said Anthropic's Mythos model was 'a good warning shot across the bow.' Google is watching Anthropic alarm governments — and wants in on that conversation.
items.tweetB: Buried in the Hassabis interview: 'My economist friends are still not taking this seriously.' The person building the thing thinks the people pricing assets are behind. That's not philosophy — that's a pricing gap.

items.title: Huawei LogicFolding: +53.5% transistor density without EUV — but spent 2x the silicon to get there
items.idea: Huawei's LogicFolding stacks two 7nm-class SMIC chips using hybrid bonding, halving the footprint to double transistor density without EUV. Kirin 2026: 155 to 238 MTr/mm² (+53.5%), +41% power efficiency, 3.1GHz. Critical caveat: Huawei spent 2x the silicon — not the same as EUV-native density. The same stacking on an EUV-class node widens the US/Taiwan lead rather than closing it. Hybrid bonding supply chain is geographically concentrated.
items.angle: Market read this as China closing the gap — hence MU and SOX ripping. Correct read: clever workaround with real cost penalties. Investable signal is hybrid bonding equipment, not Chinese fabs.
items.tweetA: Huawei's LogicFolding: stack two 7nm chips via hybrid bonding, double density. Kirin 2026 gets +53.5% MTr/mm² and +41% efficiency. Until you see the asterisk: they used 2x the silicon to get there. That's not EUV parity — it's a workaround with real cost penalties.
items.tweetB: Market read Huawei Tau Scaling as China closing the chip gap — hence MU and SOX ripping. Correct read: apply the same folding trick to TSMC's N2 node and the US lead widens, not closes. Supply chain winner is hybrid bonding equipment. Not Chinese fabs.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening (paywalled hybrid bonding supply chain post)

items.title: Web3 average network retention: 5.4%. Finance app Day 30 retention: 3%. The structural problem nobody's fixing
items.idea: Binance Research: active onchain users below 1% of global population, average Web3 network retention 5.4%. Adjust 2025: finance apps hit 13% Day 1, 6% Day 7, 3% Day 30. Crypto is worse because it lacks banking stickiness. Root cause: incentive design attracts campaign participants, not users. Only products escaping this: stablecoins — because they sit inside recurring financial behavior.
items.angle: 3% Day 30 means 97% of acquired users are wasted. Every DeFi protocol reinvents the acquisition funnel and ignores the retention drain.
items.tweetA: Web3 average network retention: 5.4%. Finance app Day 30 retention: 3%. The entire industry builds acquisition funnels for users gone in a month. Every protocol that survived long-term solved a recurring financial job — not a one-time transaction. Stablecoins are the proof.
items.tweetB: Ethena TVL: rapid rise tied to yield narrative, then collapse when users reassess. If a user can't explain why they'd return when the APY disappears, the product doesn't have retention — it has a yield-dependent audience.

charts.desc: DeFiLlama Ethena TVL chart; Glassnode holder retention framework

items.title: 56% of investors who use influencers made investment decisions based solely on influencer information
items.idea: Amundi 2025 digital investment research (11,000+ retail investors, 25 countries): 56% of investors who use influencers made a specific investment decision based solely on influencer information. 73% of retail investors use digital sources for investment information; media influencers rank as the top global source. Web3 users form conviction outside the app — through KOL posts, Telegram, creator explanations. Retention is partly a product problem and partly an information environment problem.
items.angle: Structural reason crypto narrative cycles are violent: 56% of the marginal buyer is acting on a single influencer input. When the KOL moves on, so does the capital.
items.tweetA: 56% of investors who use influencers have made a specific investment decision based SOLELY on influencer information (Amundi 2025, 11K investors). The marginal crypto buyer isn't doing research — they're executing one person's tweet. This is why narrative cycles are violent: when the KOL moves on, so does the capital.
items.tweetB: 73% of retail investors use digital sources for investment guidance. Influencers rank #1 globally. Web3 retention isn't just a product problem — it's an information environment problem. You're not building users, you're renting attention from whoever the hot KOL is this week.

charts.desc: Amundi 2025 chart showing digital source breakdown for investment decisions

items.source: Bloomberg Points of Return / John Authers
items.title: SOX 8 contributed 369 of 672 S&P 500 points in 2026 while Magnificent Six contributed only 79
items.idea: Short Mag7/long SOX from Jan 1 = +71% return. Magnificent Six (ex-NVDA) contributed 79 of the S&P's 672-point 2026 rally; SOX 8 contributed 369. Micron +19.3% in one session to $1T; SK Hynix followed hours later; both joined Samsung in the $1T club. The hyperscaler narrative is repricing: capex spenders are paying suppliers. Authers' warning: forward earnings estimates are extraordinarily uncertain in explosive growth periods. Micron at 8.42x fwd P/E vs SPX 22.15x.
items.angle: Rotation from software/platforms to hardware/supply-chain is the defining 2026 trade. The setup looks cheap until the estimates prove wrong.
items.tweetA: S&P up 672 points in 2026. Magnificent Six (ex-NVDA) contributed 79. SOX 8 contributed 369. This isn't a tech rally — it's a semiconductor rally. The hyperscalers spent a decade hoarding AI returns. Those returns are being repriced to hardware suppliers.
items.tweetB: Micron at 8.42x fwd P/E vs S&P at 22.15x. The 'chips are cyclical' narrative is dying. But it's still cheap because nobody can verify forward estimates in explosive growth. The bet is conviction on estimates, not just on the story.

charts.desc: 🔥 CHART WORTH SAVING: SOX vs Mag6 contribution chart (369 vs 79 points). Micron fwd P/E vs SPX. Rolling 12-month fwd earnings revision chart.

items.source: Bloomberg Points of Return / John Authers
items.title: Strip EM of its three biggest AI proxies and the index is back to Liberation Day lows
items.idea: Oxford Economics chart (via Clocktower's Eric Wallerstein): remove TSMC, SK Hynix, and Samsung from MSCI Emerging Markets and the group is essentially back at Liberation Day levels. The four largest MSCI EM constituents — China, South Korea, India, Taiwan — are also the most vulnerable to prolonged Hormuz closure. The AI narrative masking EM weakness is the same trade most exposed to geopolitical breakdown.
items.angle: The fragility argument for EM in one chart: chips pause = EM reverts to political risk, oil inflation, weak domestic demand. Not a timing call — a structural vulnerability.
items.tweetA: Take out TSMC, SK Hynix, and Samsung from MSCI EM and the index is flat since Liberation Day. The 'emerging market recovery' is 90% three chip stocks. Everything else — Colombia, Turkey, Indonesia, Brazil — is fighting higher oil, political chaos, and capital outflows.
items.tweetB: The four largest MSCI EM constituents are also the four most vulnerable to Hormuz closure. The AI trade and the geopolitical risk are concentrated in exactly the same names. That's not diversification — that's a correlated bet wearing two hats.

charts.desc: 🔥 CHART WORTH SAVING: EM performance with vs without AI proxies. Oxford Economics EM risk heatmap vs Nov 2022.

items.source: Bloomberg Points of Return / John Authers
items.title: EM political risk at highest since November 2022 — same intensity as post-pandemic inflation shock and Russia-Ukraine
items.idea: Oxford Economics quarterly EM survey: growth risks skewed to downside, inflation/rates feared higher for longer. Risk perceptions match November 2022 — post-pandemic inflation, aggressive CB tightening, Russia-Ukraine. Specific nodes: Colombia bonds hit hard, Turkey ousting opposition leader, Indonesia authoritarian drift, Brazil/Colombia/Peru elections, Bolivia protests. EM oil importers most exposed to Hormuz disruption.
items.angle: Hormuz dominates macro commentary but real EM fragility is domestic political risk compounding on an oil shock. Colombia is the clearest stress case.
items.tweetA: EM political risk back to November 2022 levels — that's post-pandemic inflation + aggressive CB tightening + Russia-Ukraine intensity. This time: Colombia bonds selling, Turkey ousting opposition, Indonesia going authoritarian, three LatAm elections. And Hormuz still not open.
items.tweetB: FIM Partners: EM discontent is partly deteriorating macro in oil importers. Frustration with incumbents grows harder to contain, fostering political disruption. The oil price is doing the political damage, and equity markets haven't priced it.

charts.desc: Colombia bond spread chart; Oxford Economics EM risk heatmap

items.title: US + Israel airstrikes on Iranian missile sites and Hormuz boats overnight — equities at records, crude -2.3%
items.idea: US and Israeli forces conducted overnight airstrikes targeting Iranian missile sites and Hormuz boats (CENTCOM: 'self-defense'). 7 US casualties in Iranian retaliatory attacks; 6 Air Force airmen died in KC-135 crash in Iraq. Iran FM: 'consensus reached on many topics but signing not imminent.' Three LNG tankers still passed Hormuz over the weekend. Market response: WTI -2.3%, S&P and Nasdaq at records. The 'deal anyway' narrative so locked in that actual kinetic action is being discounted.
items.angle: Non-reaction to overnight airstrikes IS the signal. When narrative locks in this hard, the tail risk isn't the baseline — it's everything that breaks the narrative.
items.tweetA: US + Israel airstrikes on Iranian missile sites and Hormuz boats overnight. 7 US casualties. S&P closed at a record. Crude -2.3%. The market has decided there's a deal regardless of what happens on the ground. When a narrative locks in that hard, the tail isn't the baseline — it's everything that breaks it.
items.tweetB: Three LNG tankers passed through Hormuz over the weekend while US and Israeli aircraft were striking nearby. The strait is simultaneously a war zone and a functioning trade route. The market prices that both remain true. The question is how long both can be true at the same time.

items.title: Samsung 45,000-worker strike starts TODAY — biggest semiconductor work stoppage in history at NVDA's $91B demand moment
items.idea: Samsung's 45,000-worker strike began today for 18 days, hitting HBM/DRAM production simultaneously with NVDA's $91B Q2 demand guide and Micron's first day as a $1T company. Micron's entire 2026 HBM supply is already sold out. The memory oligopoly (Samsung/SK Hynix/Micron) has pricing power intact. This is a direct supply constraint at peak demand. SK Hynix and Micron are the immediate beneficiaries.
items.angle: 18 days of Samsung strike at peak HBM demand = supply squeeze that validates every bull thesis on Micron and SK Hynix simultaneously.
items.tweetA: Samsung's 45,000-worker strike started today. 18 days. Biggest semiconductor work stoppage in history — timed to NVDA's $91B Q2 demand guide and Micron's first day as a $1T company. Samsung HBM goes offline just as demand hits all-time high. SK Hynix and Micron are the math.
items.tweetB: Micron's entire 2026 HBM supply: already sold out. UBS tripled the PT to $1,625. Now Samsung's 45K workers are striking for 18 days. The memory market has never had three simultaneous demand-plus-supply tailwinds this concentrated.

items.title: April PCE expected +0.4% MoM Thursday — Pettit's 7,700 ceiling leaves only 2% upside from today's record
items.idea: BofA forecasts April PCE at +0.4% headline MoM and +0.3% core — biggest tape risk of the week. Morgan Stanley's Pettit: 'yields higher + inflation higher + flatter curve = no higher multiple' — 7,700 YE target is 2% from the current record 7,519. Kevin Warsh, Day 4 as Fed Chair, faces first FOMC June 16-17. Bond market diverging: 10Y at 4.50%, 30Y above 5% despite WTI crude collapsing on Iran peace narrative.
items.angle: Bond yields not following oil lower: inflation being repriced as structural, not energy-driven. If PCE prints hot Thursday, Warsh's first act becomes a hawkish credibility test.
items.tweetA: S&P at record 7,519. PCE expected hot Thursday. Morgan Stanley's ceiling: 7,700 — 2.4% from here. Yields haven't moved despite oil collapsing on Iran peace. Bond market is telling you something equity market doesn't want to hear: inflation doesn't disappear just because crude does.
items.tweetB: Kevin Warsh, Day 4 as Fed Chair. Inherits: record equities, PCE expected hot, 30Y above 5%, an Iran deal that could crater energy revenue, retail inflows into AI stocks at ATH. First FOMC is June 17. The easy part of his tenure ended before it started.

items.title: NVDA $81.6B Q1 (+85% YoY), Q2 guide $91B; hyperscaler capex revised UP to $725B for 2026
items.idea: NVDA Q1: $81.6B revenue (+85% YoY), Q2 guide $91B, $80B buyback, 25x dividend hike. NVDA-Anthropic partnership 'scaling very, very quickly.' Cisco AI orders pipeline raised to $9B (from $5B). Hyperscaler 2026 capex revised UP to $725B plus $30B sovereign AI. At $91B quarterly run rate, NVDA is approaching $360B annualized — larger than most S&P 500 companies' entire market cap.
items.angle: Capex keeps going up, not down. $725B hyperscaler + $30B sovereign AI is the largest single-year tech infrastructure commitment in history.
items.tweetA: NVDA Q1: $81.6B (+85% YoY). Q2 guide: $91B. Annualized approaching $360B. Hyperscaler capex revised UP to $725B. Cisco AI pipeline: $9B (was $5B). The question isn't whether demand is real — it's whether anyone can build fast enough to meet it.
items.tweetB: NVDA guided $91B for Q2 and announced an $80B buyback + 25x dividend hike in the same breath. A company simultaneously investing in its future AND returning capital at scale. The $725B capex revision UP means the hyperscalers are paying for all of it.

items.title: Crypto diverges hard from equities: BTC/ETH/SOL red on record-high stocks while whale accumulation hits yearly high
items.idea: S&P and Nasdaq at all-time highs; BTC (-1.09%), ETH (-1.59%), SOL (-1.38%) all fell. BTC ETF 6-day outflow streak: $1.55B total; ETH ETF 10-day outflow streak. Simultaneously: entities holding 1,000+ BTC reached 1,282 wallets (yearly high), whale-vs-retail delta divergence strongest since November 2024. Last time this divergence was this extreme, BTC doubled over the following 6 months. The peace-dividend oil collapse supports stocks but not crypto.
items.angle: Retail exiting while institutional wallets accumulate at yearly highs — against equity records and ETF outflows — is the setup for a delayed crypto catch-up trade.
items.tweetA: Equities at all-time records. Crypto: BTC -1%, ETH -1.6%, SOL -1.4%. Six-day BTC ETF outflows. Ten-day ETH ETF outflows. Meanwhile whale wallets hit a yearly high in accumulation and the whale-vs-retail delta is the strongest since November 2024. What whales do at retail lows is usually the trade.
items.tweetB: Entities holding 1,000+ BTC reached 1,282 wallets — a 2026 high — on a day retail was selling into BTC ETF outflows. The last time this divergence was this extreme was November 2024. You know what happened after November 2024.

items.title: May 29 BTC options expiry: $6B notional, $82K call concentration vs $75K max pain
items.idea: May 29 BTC options expiry: $6B notional, call concentration at $82K vs max pain at $75K. The 200-day MA at $82,228 has been untouched for 7 weeks. Deribit put/call ratio at 0.41 (bullish). Strategy (MSTR) holds 818,334 BTC at $75,700 average cost basis, defended at $74,500 weekend low. 50-day MA at $75K. The $82K level is both the options wall and the 200DMA — two independent magnets for the same level.
items.angle: Gamma structure around 200DMA makes $82K a magnet heading into Thursday. Cleanest near-term BTC setup of Q2.
items.tweetA: May 29 BTC options: $6B notional. Max pain at $75K. Call concentration at $82K — which is also the 200-day MA untouched for 7 weeks. Two independent reasons for the same target. The gamma structure makes $82K a magnet heading into Thursday.
items.tweetB: Strategy: 818,334 BTC at $75,700 average cost. Saylor defended $74,500 floor over the weekend. 50-day MA is $75K. 200-day is $82K. Options gamma is at $82K. These aren't coincidences — they're the architecture of the next move.

items.title: Intuit -15% on 17% workforce cut: AI displacement moving from narrative to income statement
items.idea: Intuit is down 15% after announcing a 17% workforce reduction explicitly framed in AI displacement terms, not business weakness. The company behind TurboTax and QuickBooks is eliminating 17% of its people to deploy AI-native workflows. This comes as NVDA prints +85% YoY revenue growth and hyperscaler capex hits $725B. AI displacement is moving from think-piece narrative to corporate earnings reality — and the market is re-rating the revenue model, not just applauding the efficiency.
items.angle: Intuit -15% on AI-driven cuts signals the market is questioning whether AI efficiency gains accrue to shareholders or just shrink margins in a different way.
items.tweetA: Intuit -15% on a 17% workforce cut explicitly framed as AI displacement. Not a restructuring. Not a cycle. The company that does your taxes is replacing 17% of its people with AI workflows. This is white-collar displacement moving from narrative to income statement.
items.tweetB: NVDA at $360B annualized run rate, hyperscaler capex $725B, and now Intuit cutting 17% of staff for AI. The build-out and the displacement are happening simultaneously. The market cheers NVDA and punishes Intuit. Both reactions are correct.

items.title: SpaceX $1.5T IPO (June 11-12) + Cerebras + OpenAI: generational IPO supply wave hitting record euphoria
items.idea: SpaceX S-1 absorbing at $1.5T+ valuation, June 11-12 pricing target. Index funds can't say no (Bloomberg/Matt Levine: passive forced buying at whatever price). Cerebras and OpenAI both queuing up — largest simultaneous AI-themed IPO supply event in history, all pricing into record equity prices and peak AI narrative. The sovereign wealth and institutional demand side looks strong; retail durability post-PCE Thursday is the variable.
items.angle: Three of the largest IPOs in history pricing simultaneously into peak AI narrative = either distribution top or bull cycle's next catalyst. Passive forced-buying dynamic for SpaceX specifically is worth sizing.
items.tweetA: SpaceX ($1.5T, June 11-12) + Cerebras + OpenAI all pricing into record market euphoria simultaneously. Largest AI-themed IPO supply wave in history. Index funds literally cannot say no to SpaceX. The tape will absorb it — until it can't.
items.tweetB: When SpaceX joins major indexes, passive funds are forced to buy at whatever price the market sets. Add OpenAI and Cerebras queuing behind it. This is either the IPO wave that extends the bull cycle — or the distribution top. Both are worth pricing.

items.title: KOSPI: SK Hynix 30% + Samsung 25% = 55% AI concentration; BOK new governor meets TOMORROW — rate hike at 1% odds
items.idea: Korean equities have gone parabolic: SK Hynix ~30% of MSCI South Korea, Samsung ~25%, totaling over 55% in AI/chip names. Speculators have simultaneously bid up Korean apartment prices. Geopolitics-driven commodity spikes are pushing PPI inflation — meaning the Bank of Korea is leaning toward rate hikes. The new BOK governor holds his first meeting May 28 (tomorrow, Korea time). Polymarket puts rate hike odds at just 1%. A hawkish surprise would rattle Korean stocks — which are a direct microcosm of the US AI trade — and could ripple into US tech immediately.
items.angle: 1% priced odds for BOK hike = tail risk with zero premium. KOSPI is 55% the same trade as US AI semis. New governor wanting to establish inflation-fighting credibility on day one is a real risk.
items.tweetA: SK Hynix is 30% of the MSCI South Korea index. Samsung is 25%. Over 55% of the index is a direct AI chip bet. Korean apartments are being bid up by the same narrative. The BOK's new governor meets TOMORROW. Rate hike odds: 1%. The tail risk has no premium.
items.tweetB: Korean stocks are a microcosm of US tech: 55% concentration in two chip names, parabolic move, speculation spilling into real estate, PPI inflation rising. The difference: the BOK can actually hike. A surprise tomorrow ripples directly into US AI stocks. Nobody's positioned for it.

charts.desc: 🔥 CHART WORTH SAVING: KOSPI parabolic chart driven by SK Hynix/Samsung concentration — direct visual analog to US AI concentration risk

items.source: Bloomberg Points of Return / John Authers
items.title: Sonny Rollins dies at 96 — last survivor of 'A Great Day in Harlem' — on the eve of Miles Davis's 100th birthday
items.idea: Jazz great Sonny Rollins died at 96 on Monday — the day before what would have been Miles Davis's 100th birthday. Rollins was the last surviving musician from the iconic 1958 photograph 'A Great Day in Harlem,' which captured 57 jazz legends. With Rollins's death, the entire generation that made jazz simultaneously avant-garde and mass culture has passed. Ted Gioia notes in his appreciation: this is the closing of a musical era that also coincides with the centenary of the art form's greatest practitioner.
items.angle: The last witness to a golden age passing while the next gold rush is at full frenzy. The jazz parallel to today: a form that was technical, commercial, and culturally dominant simultaneously — then got fragmented by the next wave.
items.tweetA: Sonny Rollins died at 96 yesterday — the last survivor of the 57 jazz musicians photographed in 'A Great Day in Harlem' in 1958. He died the day before Miles Davis's 100th birthday. The entire generation that made jazz both avant-garde and mass culture simultaneously is now gone.
items.tweetB: The 1958 'A Great Day in Harlem' photo: 57 jazz legends, one brownstone, one moment when an entire art form peaked simultaneously. Rollins was the last one alive. Something about watching the last witness of a golden age pass while the next gold rush is still in full frenzy.
